coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible and nuclear penetrating carbon quantum dots for photoresistive bioimaging applications in animal cell lines
coredata.dc:creator: Praseetha, Prabhakaran Kala
Chandran, Parvathy
Devi, Vasudevan Aruna
Alexander, Princy
Vijayakumar, Subramanian
coredata.dc:description: Organic dyes used today for imaging the diseased cells are toxic and photobleach easily on light exposures. The exclusive spectral properties and the ultra small size of the Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) exert assistance in the vast exploration of the cytoplasmic and nuclear regions of human cells. The present work was intended to synthesize carbon quantum dots from Citrus medica juice and its peel by using pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods. The CQDs exhibited blue to green fluorescence at different excitation and emission wavelengths. The fluorescent particles could easily bind to the protein- Bovine Serum Albumen proving its biological membrane binding properties through the quenching studies with increasing concentrations of CQDs. The produced CQDs also exerted a higher antimicrobial effect against both Gram positive and negative bacteria. The biocompatibility of the CQDs was evident through the in vivo toxicity analyses and the nuclear penetration properties. Thus the nanomaterials could be graded superior to the organic stains used conventionally on the fibroblast cells. This study explores the possibilities of synthesizing fluorescent, ultrasmall, biocompatible CQDs that can easily enter the diseased tissues, favors nuclear membrane penetration and assist diagnosis through bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Pyrolysis
Hydrothermal
Bioimaging
Nuclear penetration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs using pyrolysis method
Synthesis of CQDs using hydrothermal method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the total study.
CQD samples visualized in normal and UV light.
UV–Vis spectrum of the synthesized CQDs through Hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods.
TEM analysis for the produced CQDs through pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods.
PL spectrum of produced CQDs (hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods) when excited at different wavelengths.
Antibacterial analysis of CQDs using (a) Staphylococcus aureus, (b) Bacillus subtilis, (c) Escherichia coli and (d) Proteus vulgaris.
PL spectrum of BSA and BSA with CQDs at different concentrations at excitations of 290 nm.
Visualization of Zebra fish embryos at 0dpf.
Visualization of Zebra fish embryos at 48 hpf.
Nuclear penetration and staining of fibroblast cells (a) control cells (b) with CQDs, cytoplasmic membrane dye and DAPI dye (c) DAPI dye excited at 405 nm laser.
Zones of inhibition produced by the bacterial species tested.
Mortality test of zebra fish embryo at 3–4 hpf.
Heart beat rate analysis of zebra fish embryo at 72 hpf.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible and nuclear penetrating carbon quantum dots for photoresistive bioimaging applications in animal cell lines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil, Kanyakumari District, Tamil Nadu 629180, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology, Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education,Kumaracoil, Kanyakumari District, Tamil Nadu, India- 629180

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG and Research Department of Botany, A.V.V.M Sri Pushpam College (Autonomous), Poondi, (Affiliated to Bharathidasan University) Tamil Nadu, 613503, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: PG and Research Department of Botany
A.V.V.M Sri Pushpam College (Autonomous), Poondi, (Affiliated to Bharathidasan University) Tamil Nadu

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG and Research Department of Botany, A.V.V.M Sri Pushpam College (Autonomous), Poondi, (Affiliated to Bharathidasan University)Tamil Nadu, India- 613503

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic dyes used today for imaging the diseased cells are toxic and photobleach easily on light exposures. The exclusive spectral properties and the ultra small size of the Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) exert assistance in the vast exploration of the cytoplasmic and nuclear regions of human cells. The present work was intended to synthesize carbon quantum dots from Citrus medica juice and its peel by using pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods. The CQDs exhibited blue to green fluorescence at different excitation and emission wavelengths. The fluorescent particles could easily bind to the protein- Bovine Serum Albumen proving its biological membrane binding properties through the quenching studies with increasing concentrations of CQDs. The produced CQDs also exerted a higher antimicrobial effect against both Gram positive and negative bacteria. The biocompatibility of the CQDs was evident through the in vivo toxicity analyses and the nuclear penetration properties. Thus the nanomaterials could be graded superior to the organic stains used conventionally on the fibroblast cells. This study explores the possibilities of synthesizing fluorescent, ultrasmall, biocompatible CQDs that can easily enter the diseased tissues, favors nuclear membrane penetration and assist diagnosis through bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials find a reasonable demand in different areas of research like in the biomedical field, in catalytic process, targeted drug delivery, space, etc. because of the remarkable properties to fit in diverse fields (Feng et al., 2014). The shape, size and the type of nanoparticles decide the frequency of light emission and have peculiar optical and electrical properties that exhibit higher quantum yields and versatility than their micro sized counterparts (Major et al., 2016; Taranova et al., 2015).

Carbon quantum dots are dimensionless or zero dimension particles with the particle size <10 nm (Liu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2010; Das et al., 2018). CQDs possess finely changeable fluorescence characteristics, making it to be utilized in various fields of research like in bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensors, optronics, solar cells, etc. (Yue et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2013). The characteristics of CQDs such as chemically inert nature, easy alteration, higher solubility, biologically compatible nature, reduced toxicity and greater resistance against optical bleaching paved way for the easy adaptation of it in various commercial fields (Ramanan et al., 2016; Kumawat et al., 2017; Baker and Baker, 2010).

Citrus medica is a fruit bearing plant of high medicinal value possessing various bioactive agents helpful in curing many ailments. In this specified research work, the CQDs are produced from Citrus medica juice and peel as the organic precursor molecule, since citric acid is one of the efficient sources for CQD synthesis (Dubey et al., 2014). This fruit belongs to the family, Rutaceae and possess extensive biological properties such as antidote, antibiotic and also used to treat various biological disorders with greater efficacy. Both the methods (pyrolysis and hydrothermal) are proven to be a cost- effective and non-toxic mode of producing uniformly dispersed CQDs (Wang et al., 2017) Fig. 1 depicts the overall process of the study done and the results derived from it.

The Citrus medica fruit was collected locally from the market and washed with deionized water. The fruit peel and the juice was collected separately and stored for the analysis. All other chemicals used were of analytical grade obtained from Sigma Aldrich chemicals, India.

UV–Visible spectroscopy (SYSTRONICS Double beam UV-VIS Spectrophotometer: 2202, scan mode- 200 to 800 nm), Transmission Electron Microscopic analysis (JEOL JEM-1210), and Photoluminescence analysis with spectroflurometer (ELICO SL174, India) were used for the characterization of the synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs using pyrolysis method
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were produced separately both from the Citrus medica juice and peel. The fruit juice was squeezed out and the extract was filtered by using filter paper (Whatmann No.1). From the obtained extract, 50 ml of the extract was heated in a round bottomed flask at 180 °C for 60 min (Dong et al., 2012). A color change to an orange liquid was obtained. Size control and uniform properties are the important features of pyrolysis method. The extract was then centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 30 min and the supernatant was filtered out to remove larger sized particles and stored for further analysis. This solution produced from the fruit was coded as CMJP and the obtained pure solution from the fruit peel was coded as CMPP. All the samples were stored for further characterization studies and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs using hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: As wide range of pH and photoluminescence has been reported through one stage hydrothermal methods, this process was also used for the CQDs synthesis (Yue et al., 2014). 40 ml of juice extracted from the fruit was diluted with 40 ml of deionized water. It was then transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 4 h and was allowed to cool under room temperature. A brown colored solution obtained was filtered using filter paper (Whatmann No.1) and stored for further analysis. The sample obtained from the fruit was coded as CMJH and those from the peel was coded as CMPH. The samples thus prepared were individually stored for further analysis.

The antibacterial analysis (Premanathan et al., 2011) was carried out with two Gram- positive (Bacillus subtilis (G+), Staphylococcus aureus (G+)) as well as two Gram - negative bacteria (Escherichia coli (G-), Proteus vulgaris (G-)) using agar well plate method. The antibacterial analysis was studied using sterilized Nutrient Agar medium and the bacterial cultures were swabbed over the plated media separately. The CQD samples were added to it and the zones of inhibition were examined with positive control, Streptomycin after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C.

For quenching study, 0.015 g of BSA was dissolved in 25 ml of deionized water by stirring for 10 min in a magnetic stirrer. It was used as the standard solution and the photoluminescence spectrum was measured. Meanwhile, an increasing concentration of 10 μl to 50 μl of each sample (CMJH, CMPH, CMJP and CMPP) was added to 2 ml of BSA solution taken in four test tubes. The setup was kept undisturbed for 15 min and the fluorescence spectra were analyzed. The samples were excited at 290 nm and their respective emission ranges were observed.

Toxicity analysis was conducted in the embryo of zebra fish. The mortality and heart beat rate were analyzed after the breeding period and the mortality tests were carried out at 3–4 days post fertilization (dpf) and heart beat rate was noted at 72 h post fertilization (hpf). Briefly, 400 μl of Embryo Rearing Solution (ERS) was added along with 100 μl of the samples and 3 embryos in each well for the stock preparation. It was then serially diluted by 100 μl of stock with 400 μl of ERS along with 3 embryos. A manual method of heart beat rate measurement with a simple microscope that had a CCD attachment. Photographic and video records were used that helped to visualize the images that were further analyzed with Image–J software. The mortality and heart beat rate were subsequently recorded.

The penetration of CMPP sample (less toxic CQD) into the nuclei of the fibroblast cells (maintained in Fibroblast growth media, Sigma aldrich) were analyzed (Escarate et al., 2007) by seeding the cells in 24-well plates and incubated until cell growth reaches log phase for 24 h. The cells were then washed with 1× PBS (phosphate buffer saline) and then fixed with 50 μl of methanol and water (1:1) under 20 °C for 10 min. The cells were again rinsed and stained with 100 μl (1 μg/ml) of DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) dye, red cytoplasmic membrane dye and with 50 μM solution of CMPP-CQDs. After maintaining in the dark for 30 min at 37 °C, the excess CQDs were removed and 20 μl of PBS: glycerin (1:1) was added to it. The cells were then observed and imaged using confocal microscopy (Olympus FV3000) for the comparison of CQD penetration and nuclear staining with the DAPI dye.

The emission of green luminescence by the CQDs under UV irradiation and the absence of it under natural light primarily confirmed the presence CQDs in the sample mixture. This emission is due to the photon excitation and absorption of UV radiation leading to the reversion of electrons to the ground state (Fig. 2 ). The work exactly coincides with the studies done by who synthesized luminescent Carbon Quantum dots through hydrothermal method.

The absorbance characteristics of the produced CQDs are examined through UV- Vis spectrophotometer. The CQDs coded as CMJP, CMJH, CMPP and CMPH exhibits maximum absorbance at 262 & 292 nm, 266.8 nm, 250 & 310 nm and 263.2 nm correspondingly (Fig. 3 ). This absorbance spectrum was obtained due to the excitation of electrons to a greater energy level. The band corresponds to the transition of n–π* and π–π* of –C=O (oxygen at non-bonding state) and CC (sp2) associated bonds of carbon nanoparticles. The obtained results were very much correlated with the works done by Wang et al. (2017).

The TEM analysis revealed the nanosize of the produced CQDs and the uniform dispersion of CQDs. The average diameter of CMJF- CQD was found to be 2 nm whereas the average diameters of CMJH, CMPP and CMPH were found to be 1 nm, 0.8 nm and 1 nm respectively. The spherical shapes of the CQDs were visually confirmed using image J software. The surface functionalization characteristics were visible through this analysis in the sample: CMJH whereas the other particles focused displayed to have a smooth surface (Fig. 4 ). Though the average size of the particles identified through TEM was ranged between 0.5 and 5 nm, the particles recorded from CMJH were found to be larger compared to the other samples. This characteristic is attributed to the functionalization properties and the adherence of bioactive molecules to the spherical surface that is responsible for the bioactivity exhibited by the sample. The size ranges recorded through the TEM studies indicated that the results were exactly comparable to the CQDs produced by Gao et al. (2020) which turned out to occur between 0.5 nm to 5 nm and the synthesis was accomplished through hydrothermal process.

Photoluminescence, the unique characteristic of CQDs, produced fluorescence at different excitation and emission wavelengths. The CMJP exhibited fluorescence of 9967 mV at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm and emission wavelength of 449.5 nm. Similarly, CMJH exhibits fluorescence of 7221.5 mV at an excitation and emission wavelength of 370 and 473 nm accordingly. Whereas, CMPP and CMPH has a fluorescence of 3982.1 and 6955.2 mV at an excitation & emission wavelengths of 390 & 469 nm and 400 & 485 nm correspondingly. Among the produced CQDs, CMJF is found to produce highest fluorescence than the other CQDs as indicated by the Fig. 5 . The values and the strong fluorescence recorded from the samples obtained were matching with the data recorded by Anastasia et al. (2020) for utilizing CQDs towards environmental and health related applications. The photostable property exhibited by the CQDs has been proven through many earlier studies in illuminating the deeper tissues thus helping to track diseased cells and organelles.

The zone of inhibition in different bacterial cultures is given in the Table 1 and Fig. 6 . It denotes that the samples, CMPP and CMPH exhibit no anti-bacterial effect, whereas the other CQDs have a reasonable antibacterial effect on both the Gram- positive as well as negative bacteria. The CQDs coded as CMJP projects a maximum antibacterial effect with a zone of inhibition of 17 mm on E. coli and 19 mm for other Gram - positive and negative bacteria. The value obtained for the zone of inhibition of samples, CMJP and CMJH resides in the sensitive region, thereby, confirming that the CQDs possess good antibacterial effect selectively against Gram+ve and Gram-ve bacteria. This peculiar property is pertained to the capacity of CQDs to easily penetrate the cell membranes and hinder the microbial existence through selective toxicity. These preferred toxicity, probably due to generation of ROS and membrane disintegration to selective microorganisms is exactly similar to those exhibited by the CQDs synthesized by thin film preparations to enhance the antibacterial property recorded earlier (Omran et al., 2019).

The quenching effect of synthesized CQDs was analyzed by using BSA (0.015 g/25 ml) as a standard protein in which the PL spectrum of BSA was excited at 290 nm exhibiting an emission spectrum at 350 nm. Similarly, by analyzing the PL spectrum of the CQDs along with the BSA protein that was excited at 290 nm, showed that the fluorescence of the CQD was found to decrease by the increase in the concentration of the CQDs. This confirmed that the protein molecules there by act as masking agents. It approves the capability of CQDs to interact with the human protein molecules as BSA shares maximum similarity with the other major human proteins. The conjugation process of CQDs with the proteins favors the fluorescence decay recorded during the study and is well correlated to the results obtained by Poojan et al. (2018) (Fig. 7 ). The quenching of fluorescence by the CQDs on combining with BSA supports the capability of protein conjugation by the CQDs on penetrating the cell membrane and the endocytosis during targeting. This property defines the easy entry of CQDs into the living cells.

Zebra fishes do maintain perfect homology to humans and hence toxicity analysis with zebra fishes has been practiced for accurate results. Tables 2 and 3
provide the data on the mortality and heart beat rate of the zebra fish embryo when subjected to the produced CQDs. The data presented the number of embryos decreased after 2 h, 6 h, 24 h and 48 h. It implies that in the sample CMPP incubated with the embryos could support the embryo survival after 48 h and the toxic range is less compared with the other samples. The Fig. 8 and Fig. 9 implies the embryo images at 0 h and 48 h.

By analyzing the heartbeat rate of the embryos after 24 h, the sample CMPP and CMJP shows less variation by comparing it at 2 h time period. This implies that the sample CMPP is highly applicable for the biomedical applications including implants as they possess less hindrance to the typical metabolism of the embryo (Tables 2 and 3). The results substantiate the influence of CQDs on Zebra fish embryos and hence promote curiosity towards further research and development in biocompatible products from CQDs (Chih-Yu et al., 2021).

Cellular and nuclear membrane penetration is easily possible for these CQDs due to their ultra small size and biomolecular binding efficiency. These CQDs are also found to possess higher photostability than the organic fluorescent dyes like DAPI. The confocal imaging of the bound CQDs to the nuclear membrane reveals perfect binding morphology, which may be further used for photoresistive bioimaging of diseased tissues for the observation of nuclear activities. The Fig. 10 reveals the penetration of the CQDs along with the other standard dyes. The study could be equated to the results produced by Donate et al. (2019), who produced highly photostable CQDs that were stable upto 2 h of light exposure and were below the size of 10 nm. CQDs can support single particle tracking, molecular diagnostic and can help to track the dynamic behaviour of individual drug moieties on reaching a cellular system.

In conclusion, the carbon quantum dots produced from Citrus medica through pyrolysis and hydrothermal method was proved to be efficient in cell and nuclear penetration and for visualizing the animal cells and organelles. The TEM analysis revealed the uniform dispersion of CQDs showing their nanosize of less than 10 nm. The property of photoluminescence was projected out through their excitation and emission wavelengths. The carbon dots produced through pyrolysis method from the fruit peel were found to be biocompatible and were antibacterial in nature. This study can further assist research in the intra-cellular/nuclear tracking of the biochemical changes that occurs inside the human cells.
Praseetha (PK) has design the work, monitor the overall setup including writing the paper, all authors has been helped to finish the paper.
Parvathy Chandran (PC) has analyze the Characterizations.
Vasudevan Aruna Devi (VAD) has analyze the biocompatible.
Princy Alexandera (PA) has monitor the carbon quantum activities.
Vijayakumar S (VS) has analyze biochemical constituents.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no implicit conflicts of interests in the author's viewpoint.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly grateful to the funding provided by DST (SR/FST/ET-1/2017/87) and TNSCST/STP-PRG/AR/2018-2019. The authors thank the management of Noorul Islam Centre for Higher Education, Kumaracoil for the full-fledged support provided to execute and complete the research work successfully.
